questions.text: What is the purpose of thermal paste in a computer?

options.text: To glue the CPU to the motherboard
To improve heat transfer between the CPU and the heat sink

questions.explanation: Thermal paste fills microscopic gaps between the CPU and heat sink surfaces, ensuring efficient heat transfer from the processor to the cooling solution.

questions.text: Which network device operates at Layer 3 of the OSI model?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network layer) of the OSI model. They use IP addresses to route packets between different networks.

options.text: A combination of striping and mirroring

questions.explanation: RAID 1 (mirroring) creates an exact copy of data on two or more drives. If one drive fails, the system continues operating from the mirror, providing redundancy.

questions.text: What does DHCP stand for and what does it do?

options.text: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol — automatically assigns IP addresses to devices
Direct Hardware Control Protocol — manages hardware devices
Data Handling and Communication Protocol — encrypts network data
Distributed Host Computing Platform — manages cloud resources

questions.explanation: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters to devices, eliminating the need for manual network configuration.

questions.text: What type of connector is commonly used for Ethernet networking?
questions.explanation: RJ-45 is the standard connector used for Ethernet networking cables (Cat5, Cat5e, Cat6, etc.). RJ-11 is used for telephone connections.

questions.text: What is the function of the motherboard in a computer?

options.text: To serve as the main circuit board connecting all components
To display graphics on the screen

questions.explanation: The motherboard is the main circuit board that connects and allows communication between all computer components including CPU, RAM, storage, and expansion cards.

questions.text: What is the difference between SSD and HDD?

options.text: SSDs use spinning disks; HDDs use flash memory
SSDs use flash memory with no moving parts; HDDs use spinning magnetic disks
They are the same technology with different names
SSDs are only used in servers; HDDs are for personal computers

questions.explanation: SSDs (Solid State Drives) use NAND flash memory with no moving parts, offering faster read/write speeds. HDDs (Hard Disk Drives) use spinning magnetic platters and are generally cheaper per gigabyte.

questions.text: What does RAM stand for and what is its purpose?

options.text: Read Access Memory — stores data permanently
Random Access Memory — provides temporary fast storage for active programs and data
Rapid Application Manager — speeds up software installations
Remote Access Module — enables network connections

questions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile memory that temporarily stores data and instructions for programs currently in use, allowing the CPU to access them quickly.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a power supply unit (PSU)?

options.text: To store electricity for later use
To convert AC power from the wall outlet to DC power for computer components

questions.explanation: The PSU converts alternating current (AC) from the wall outlet into the various direct current (DC) voltages needed by the computer's internal components.

questions.text: Which type of expansion slot is most commonly used for modern graphics cards?
questions.explanation: PCIe (PCI Express) x16 is the standard expansion slot used for modern graphics cards, providing high bandwidth needed for GPU communication with the CPU and RAM.

options.text: A physical address printed on the network card
A unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network

questions.explanation: An IP (Internet Protocol) address is a unique numerical label assigned to each device connected to a computer network, used for identification and location addressing.

questions.text: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?

options.text: TCP is wireless; UDP is wired
TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and faster but less reliable
TCP is for email only; UDP is for web browsing only
They are the same protocol with different versions

questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) establishes a connection and guarantees delivery with error checking. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) sends data without establishing a connection, trading reliability for speed.

questions.text: What is a subnet mask used for?

options.text: To determine which portion of an IP address refers to the network and which refers to the host

questions.explanation: A subnet mask divides an IP address into the network portion and the host portion, helping devices determine whether a destination is on the local network or a remote one.

questions.text: What port number does HTTPS use by default?
questions.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443 by default. Regular HTTP uses port 80, FTP uses port 21, and SMTP uses port 25.

options.text: Translates domain names into IP addresses

questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (like example.com) into IP addresses that computers use to identify each other on a network.
